replies.author: @ChrisisisB
replies.text:
6:00 perfect excuse, give the publishers what amounts to an ultimatum, and then blame them for choosing the least bad choice, based on your own facts as you presented to them.

Not only does Martin lack the  motivation, the last two books meandered all over the place. A decent editor with a backbone - yes hard with a successful author - could have cut them down to one book. Cut out all the Dorne stuff (Arienne, Darkstar, Quentin) except the Viper, cut out all the Brienne travels across Westeros, halve the Mereen chapter. All of those chapters only add worldbuilding and could have been put into a later book if important but more likely a ”Tales of Westeros” separate book. All of this ”I wrote X hundred of pages, but still far from done” just signals IMO more meandering, more useless POVs that add nothing to the actual story.

replies.text: ​@iepvienredstoneHuy007I never implied that it didn't. I know that Tolkien hated allegory, but I think it's evident that the horror of war, particularly mechanised was on a grand scale, along with the huge industrialisation of Britain was something horrifying and depressing to a man that loved nature, song, poetry and a deep love of language. He was however always adamant that the lord of the rings was and never should be seen as an allegory of his life or the world he lived in. He was far more excited about the story that he'd created than people trying to pin it on his personal experience.

replies.author: @frocat5163
replies.text:
*"It was 19 years from Tolkien fighting in the battle of the Somme. To having written the Hobbit and the first two volumes of LOTR and constructing all the languages, "*
LOL. Not even close. The Battle of the Somme was fought in 1916. _The Hobbit_ was published in September 1937. He submitted the manuscript for publication in October 1936. He had completed a semi-final draft of _The Fellowship of the Ring_ in 1948. He had worked on the manuscript for the entire volume, i.e. all six books of Fellowship, Towers, and King, simultaneously, it wasn't published until 1954 - 1955. And the remainder of his legendarium was under almost constant revision over the rest of his life.

So, no, he absolutely *HAD NOT* written _"The Hobbit_ and the first two volumes of LOTR and constructing all the languages," 19 years after the Somme. That would have been 1936. Not even _The Hobbit_ was completed in 1936.

I don't know what you have said related to Tolkien, my comment was not about you. I bought and watched Game of Thrones as well as LOTR, but I have both watched and read LOTR more than once, and have yet found the motivation to go back to Game of Thrones. I just don't think the plot or story of Game of Thrones is anywhere near as good, and the characters in it is also far less appealing to me than those in LOTR.

So my critique is of Martin. I think he has been arrogant in his criticism of Tolkien, I also don't think he has been honest about why he has not finished the books yet and why the end of the tv series was disappointing to so many fans.

So my comment is not about you or anyone else, it is about Martin himself!

replies.author: @cmshap
replies.text:
​​​​@FullPerspective ​I know you weren't directing your comment at me... sorry, I just worded my reply poorly. What I meant to say was this:
"I remember really loving _A Game of Thrones_ when I first read it, but never did I say [to myself], 'This guy is the next Tolkien!'."
I was just making a statement on how I compared the two writers in my mind.

Anyway, Tolkien is a better writer, but the two authors' works are quite different from one another, and each have their place. Tolkien crafted works of myth and fantasy-history, while GRRM wrote fantasy-political thrillers. I like both approaches, and GRRM's work towards the beginning of ASoIaF was better when it was more limited in scope. But then he went off the rails, and exploded the scope way beyond what was necessary. It's like he wanted his own _Silmarillion_ or something.

Personally, I quit reading when the enormity of the story just kept growing and growing, and there was no end in sight. I didn't want to be reading a series that will have no conclusion, and it's looking like the conclusion will never happen at this point.

He seems like he doesn't actually want to finish it, or even work on it anymore. He's probably opened up too many subplots and doesn't know how to resolve them all. I wouldn't either. The story is a mess.

replies.author: @FullPerspective
replies.text:
​@cmshap I have never said or thought that Martin would be the next Tolkien either. I completely agree with you that there is no indication of him being in neither his books or in the tv series based on the books.

Yes Tolkien is a better writer, I think he is a better person too. For it is only really from Martin and a few idiots who has dared call Martin the "American Tolkien" that we get the notion that Martin should be as good or better. Yes they are very different, but I still think they can be compared like you correctly do.

I don't agree that Martins work was ever better than Tolkiens, in the beginning or the end. I see the end of Martin's work to a large degree as a consequence of what is created in the beginning, so I think it is incorrect to assume Tolkien was worse and Martin better in the beginning and Tolkien better and Martin worse in the end, because the beginning was always a plan and tone set which created the end. And so I see Tolkien as just being better in general. I don't think Martin is superior to Tolkien in anything other than physical size perhaps.


I totally understand why you did not want to read a series without a conclusion. I quit watching the Walking Dead for the same reason, but had enough curiousity about Game of Thrones to stay with it at least until the point of the tv series ending, but now I have no desire to go further. I don't think Martin will ever finish the series, but even if he did, I have lost interest in reading it, for I have lost trust that the conclusion you talk about with even come either way, and that was in the writing of Martin right from the start and a negative aspect of him having too many "shock" effects, too much violence, sex and murdered heroes to make a sensible conclusion plausible. And so Martin has written himself up in a corner where he can't win and is trapped by his own creation, this is why I am sure the end and conclusion wont come. 

This is why Tolkien was always better, Tolkiens style was always to write with a end in mind, whereas Martin wrote without planning for a end and that is why his books has been inferior and not had a end. If Martin had thought about the end more then he would have realized that there was a need to limit the chaos he had in the series in the beginning which so many people loved him for. For chaos in never a ladder that leads to a good conclusion. Martin has said he was more like Tyrion in the series, and it is fitting Martin would compare himself to a character who got worse as the series progressed. For Martin is really the most like both Tyrion and Littlefinger, characters who looked like they were heading for a good end and good conclusion, but really never could because of the choices they both made right from the beginning.


You say "He seems like he doesn't actually want to finish it, or even work on it anymore. He's probably opened up too many subplots and doesn't know how to resolve them all. I wouldn't either. The story is a mess."

I agree with you 100% the story IS a mess and he doesn't want to finish it...but I don't think he ever really did want to finish it. He wanted to enjoy the ride as long as it was "fun" and then to run away when the consequence of this irresponsible fun was ending. A bit like the grasshopper and the ant story, except here there is no ants to save the grasshopper from the winter it has not prepared for....for winter is coming....and winter is the end Martin never prepared for!

replies.text:
​@xaelath7771 To quote a friend of a friend on the subject of "Writer's Block": "It is only because of the aura of mysticism that has grown up around writing that we can get away with being lazy, bored , or stuck and calling it writer’s block. Accountants don’t get to claim accountant’s block when they are having a hard time finishing an audit. If you get into an accident, you wouldn’t accept your paramedic leaving you to bleed to death, because “they just weren’t feeling it”. They aren’t allowed to have paramedic’s block.

Every person in career field X isn’t allowed to have X Block because they would get fired. Of course, when you’re a professional author and you’ve got contractual deadlines looming, try telling your editor that you’ve got writer’s block and see how that works out for you."

replies.author: @wyssmaster
replies.text:
​@spencerloolthat's actually incorrect. He owes his fans the last two books for two major reasons: 

1. He has repeatedly promised them 

2. Their financial support has gotten him to the point that he no longer needs to worry about money and can easily afford to do nothing but write.

If he'd actually finished the series and then retired, you'd be correct re: fans wanting him to write more. The fact is that his previous five books were all sold on the condition that they would be entries in a seven book series. Failing to deliver isn't just breaching his contract with the publisher, but with the fans as well. 

And regarding the "his fans are toxic because they talk about him dying!" That's ridiculous. It's entirely valid to be concerned that an obese man in his 70s will likely die before finishing two books given that it's looking more and more like we're still years away from WoW. No one (well, most people, as I can't guarantee that) is saying they HOPE he dies or that they WANT him to.

replies.text: ​@xaelath7771how exactly would he cost his publisher millions except by never finishing the books? It's guaranteed to be a best seller even if it's only mediocre

replies.author: @wyssmaster
replies.text:
​@xaelath7771my b, misread what you wrote. 

I think at this point he'd only cost himself money if he said he wouldn't ever finish it, because he'd probably be sued for breach of contract. They might lose money on collections and special editions that people wouldn't buy on principle, although there are still people that buy Rothfuss' Kingkiller stuff despite him effectively refusing to write. It wouldn't surprise me if his publisher gave his notes and draft to someone else to finish regardless of how many lawsuits it would take, unless he manages to burn everything just before or just after he dies.

author: @Valarmorgolis
text:
Back when he did his Feast of Crows  book signing tour he came to my little Northern city and appeared at a tiny book store that  maybe 75 people went. He spoke about his books and then described his writing office with all the pile notes and sticky notes of ideas he had. He described it has Messy Chaos and he wasn't sure what direction he was going to go with the story.

I knew right then that there was never going to be and end to the story of Ice and Fire. I brought a few books that night and got it signed but decades later I still haven't read the last book. Whats  was the point if I was going to be left on a cliffhanger for decades I thought. Decades later I was right.

replies.text: @theunknowncommenter725meh. The story didn’t make sense. How did Luther know Bruce was Batman? Was it just a darn good guess? Their whole coming to blows was contrived and forced and then by the time you actually get to the fight it’s basically a big nothingburger. Throw in the epic final boss fight just so you can force Wonder Woman in there and voila, a big ol crapshoot of a movie. IMO, it would have made more sense to do a proper Man of Steel sequel that had a secondary plot line that set up the events of BvS, introducing Affleck’s Bruce Wayne, him observing Clark Kent from afar and making the connection that he is Superman, introducing Lex Luthor and better explain his motivations and actually show how he came to identify both Wayne and Clark for who they really are, and end the movie on a cliffhanger setting up BvS. The payoff would have been worth it, BvS would have made more sense, and nobody would have thought BvS was a cheap commercial for Justice League. That’s just my 2¢

​@TheFredmacIt's honestly not THAT "grand." After having gotten interested in 40k lore for the past year since Space Marine 2 released, the amount of depth and detail-even 35 years ago when 40k was in its infancy and a thousand different authors didn't publish 10,000 different books-compared to ASOIAF it's like comparing a rain puddle to Lake Michigan, and only two or three guys were responsible for the lore/characters/setting etc etc (initially obviously). 

This fat turd, who's a professional writer / author for over three decades at this point, had over an entire decade just to finish ONE book and it wasn't even the LAST book-which would ideally be a lot more difficult to wrap up and bring to a coherent and logical and good conclusion. 

He got money, he got his 30 year old fantasy of being a "successful TV writer," and got tired of the ASOIAF universe in general. Plain and simple. As soon as he climbed the ladder out from under book fans, he pulled it right on up behind him without a care. replies.author: @spracketskooch
replies.text:
​@markusfreund6961 Ever read any H.P. Lovecraft? Ever seen or played Bloodborne or any Fromsoft game? They represent the antithesis of the hero's journey. You are forced into adventure by the negligence and corruption of those who came before you. Your mentors are all dead, and the only people left to guide you are untrustworthy at best. As you and your understanding grow, so does the realization of just how deeply the corruption runs, and how powerless you are in the face of things you literally cannot comprehend. In the end, you are forced to either go mad from being unable to handle reality as it is, or you are forced to make gut wrenching tradeoffs to reach an unsatisfying compromise. They are wonderful stories, whose point is to disturb and make you question. Horror exists for the same reason Halloween does. When we are allowed to play with the macabre, it becomes less frightening, and we are thus able to better adapt to reality. Every story of value does not necessarily feature the hero's journey.

There's also a war movie called Come and See, which is available for free on youtube. I would describe it as impactful. Go watch that, and tell me you can spot a hero's journey anywhere. Then tell me that it didn't impact you profoundly.

The hero's journey is our staple diet. Healthy, comforting, and satisfying. However, once in a while it enriches our lives to eat something sweet, or sour, or spicy. Even a bad meal can make you appreciate a mediocre meal, let alone a good one.

replies.text:
I subscribe to the Ethos, Logos and Pathos theory. I saw an analysis of it somewhere, can't remember where. 
Gist is, there are three leaders who will unite the people of Westeros to defeat the Night King in A Song of Ice and Fire. All three of them have different leadership styles and all exhibit elements of Ethos, Logos and Pathos, which are tools to work to convince people to your point of view. But each also strongly represent one. Jon Snow is Ethos, he will do the ethical and honourable thing and lead by example as often as he can. Daenerys Targaryen is Pathos, she will try to ignite passion in people to get them to follow her. And Tyrion Lannister is Logos, he will try to put forth the logical argument to sway people to his cause.
Now maybe George is a crazy man now but the theory claimed that these three were moving towards being these leaders through most/all of the series.

replies.author: @korinogaro1988
replies.text: There are 3 types of authors: 1. Has somewhat exact plan for the plot. Characters are tools for the plot. Therefore sometimes act in completely nonsensical way, for that character -biggest flaw of this kind of an author. Positive thing is that they can write a book every couple of months, maybe every year. 2. Has some, kinda overarching idea for the story but story serves characters. If story requires from character to act out of character than story must bend (Martin is this kind of an author). Flaw, writing books takes forever, authors like this are prone to never finishing the story. Positive thing is that books are very 'grounded'. Characters are realistic, etc. 3. Sh*t tier author. Has overarching, somewhat idea for a plot but in reality nothing serves anything. So characters act out of character and it changes the plan for the plot, other time character acts out of character because story demands it. This type is sh*t so there are no really positive sides.

replies.author: @Noqtis
replies.text:
who of you did even read the books and who is here only because of the show?
I love LOTR and asoiaf. Both have totally different strengths and weaknesses. Tolkins prose is unmatched. His world building is superb. He can describe places and make them feel as alive as people. Just to name a few.
Meanwhile Martin has totally different strengths and weaknesses. Most writer can't do 30 distinct characters. Dude goes to thousands. Even the smallest characters feel totally distinct in his work. His dialog is unmatched. Many don't even noticed, but the whole show was carried by the dialog and became shit the moment they stopped copying it word for word. All the pieces that went viral like the chaos is a ladder speech is just a shorter (a bit worse version) of what we had in the books. All of tyrions dialog people fell in love with, that was copied 1 to 1 from the books. The moment they didn't had any dialog to copy that's when the dick jokes started... 

It's also wrong to claim asoiaf doesn't have heroes. Obviously the franchise is full of heroes. Some are even totally stereotypical heroes like Eddard Stark or Jon Snow. Those characters aren't some grey in between stuff. they are very clearly on the good side and care a lot about their morality. The main difference is: usually in fantasy those heroes win in the end. this is not the case with Martin. he places them in much more realistic scenarios in which a high moral code can be a strength just like a weakness depending on the situation they are in. but what he really mastered is character development. Aragorn is from start to finish more or less the same person. The change he goes through is more an internal one. the character is written in a way so you as the reader see even before him he is the king. just like all his other characters. Gandalf will never fall to the dark side. Frodo will struggle on his way but ofc he will destroy the ring. Meanwhile Martins characters go through multiply evolution. Take a jamie lannister or Theon Greyjoy for example. Those characters start as one thing, Jamie as a villain, Theon as a friend. Then they are cough up by the plot and depending on their prior lives and current circumstances they change. If you only know the show you don't even understand those things. Jamie doesn't change that much in the show. but in the books he keeps flipping and in a very believable way. in the beginning you dislike him, but in the end you understand him and are unable to dislike him. he might not be a steropypical hero all the time but you cherish the moments he grows above himself and does stuff a hero would do. Martin is also a genius when it comes to pairing his characters with others so they grow. take tyrion for example and the arc with penny. jamie and brienne. both plots butchered in the show but are really deep in his writing. Dany, Jorah Mormont,  Sandor Clegane even arya and Sansa go through a lot of change and are totally different people in the end. 
he also has mastered resurrection in his writing by corrupting the characters of those who come back. something most writers struggle a lot with including Tolkin. 

It's like comparing tolkin and herbert. completely different styles and depending on your personal taste you will prefer one or the other. neither lotr nor dune is better or worse. same for asoiaf.

replies.text: I remember reading the first Dark Tower book called "The Gunslinger" around 1991 and found out it was to be a 7 or 8 book "magnum opus" of Stephen King's and even though I was a new DIE HARD fan, and that there were 2 more books out at the time, I straight up waited 20 years until around 2010 to go ahead and read all 8 books from start to finish knowing the tale was completed as of 2004, and getting to relive the joy of reading the first book again 2 decades later, and getting to power through all of them knowing I was getting to binge read my favorite authors biggest, and longest series ever and it was fucking awesome. That took some serious patience on my part and as of now I am so happy I didn't pick up and start ASOIAF because this is basically tragic. I know how I'd feel if King pulled some shit like this with that series, but he actually stuck to the plan, and even cheated death to be able to fish the Dark Tower series, and to has continued writing and still does so. I will quietly wait and if George finishes the last few books I will excitedly read the entire series, but until then there's no way I'm getting my heart invested into this song until it's actually been sung...

The reasons behind George not admitting that he's not going to finish the series include but are not limited to:
-Hubris
-Ego
-Arrogance
-Self indulgence
-Pride
-Not wanting to admit that he doesn't know how to tie the story altogether 
-Not allowing himself to get some help, even though monetary wise he could do it
-Not letting his publisher know (publicly) that he will not honor whatever deal they had
And the list can go on and on, but u get the basic idea.

replies.text:
​​@mcdragster6646 I can't believe you people still don't get why the ending of Game of Thrones was bad. Whether or not westeros ends up united or not is completely and utterly irrelevant. The story has literally nothing to do with what happens with the iron throne.

The entire fucking point of the story was the threat of The Others (White Walkers) and the fact that people were so wrapped up in who was going to sit on the throame that this threat was going to wipe out their entire continent. 

Jon Snow literally spent the ENTIRE story telling everyone he met that if they don't stop bickering over the throne that EVERYONE is going to die, making the ending about the political fate of Westeros means everything about Jon Snow was completely meaningless, that he was nothing more than a community organizer. Jon Snow IS the Song of Ice and Fire, and his fate is inextricably tied to the threat of the others, so making the penultimate season about The Others and the final season about whether or not Westeros is united or who sits on the throne means you (people in general, not YOU, per se) never understood the actual look of the story.

What the ending needs/needed is an explanation of The Others; who they are, what their motivation is what their relationship with humanity is and was - and Jon Needs to be the one who is at the center of it all. The iron throne and Westerosi politics needed to be done and out of the way by the penultimate season.

replies.author: @a.nonimus6705
replies.text:
Okay, this due for a few corrections. The way you talk about writers block, as though it's caused by a particular thing, indicates to me that you've never attempted to write a novel. Writers block is an inexplicable thing, which can't be attributed to anything. People have been trying to solve writers block for millenia, and determined that it is unsolvable. So, if you think you've solved it, all you've done is validate the Dunning Kreuger effect.
The next issue is that you say "Martin is a nihilist, therefore his story is meaningless"
This is perhaps the most reductive and simplistic method of judging a book by its cover. I don't know whether or not Martin is actually a nihilist, but let's assume he is. Using this logic, all Christian writers books are Christian. Yet Tolkien was Christian and LOTR is not a Christian book series. So, your statement is self-defeating. Just because someone holds a particular view, doesn't mean that everything they do must be fueled by that view. That is an extremely simplistic, black and white way of thinking, and I suggest you broaden your horizons a little. This type of thinking you're engaging in is literally what led Hollywood to become woke... (ie, every piece of work must be a direct representation of the author's worldview = woke Hollywood)

replies.author: @railsplitters79
replies.text: @a.nonimus6705 ok so now you need some correction. First my use of the term “writers block” was clearly not meant to mean in the technical sense. As he has continued to write. It was just a vernacular way to talk about his in ability to finish his this particular story. Secondly Tolkien is a Christian writer and that informs everything he writes. The lord of the rings is obviously a Christian work. Tolkien Catholic mentality is embedded in every aspect of the story. No it’s not historic fiction or even as straight forwardly allegorical as CS Lewis’ narnia books.  (Particularly the lion the witch and the wardrobe) But the themes are definitely Christian. And although Tolkien hated allegory, its not completely unfair to suggest that Aragorn is a Christ allegory. So yes being a nihilist will have an effect on the stories you write. Just as being a Christian affects the stories that Tolkien wrote.

replies.author: @JamesRDavenport
replies.text:
​@a.nonimus6705 Well I know what writer's block is. I know generally what causes it AND how to solve it and  I don't need centuries like some pretentious gnat.
Writer's block- a seeming inability to write that is really a lack of desire to do it, that's partly the cause too, as well as wanting to write something enviable to others, highly praised and worshipped for its radiant  literary brilliance.
The solution is simply for one to not care about that perfection and just write whatever you can, a little at a time. Discipline. To make yourself write it. Even if what you write stinks, that's what revising and editors are for. It's more important to finish your work than to be praised.

There is an ongoing concern in the literary industry that publishing standards have deviated in such a way that they are exclusionary towards the classics. That is, authors (other than me, since you want to pretend I know nothing) and readers are starting to realize that celebrated authors like Agatha Christie and Tolkien do not fit into the publishing requirements for today's fiction. This is a highly contentious issue right now in publishing, because people are (rightfully) worried that we're encouraging future generations to be less literate.

This is my perspective: he wrote himself into a corner.

He can't do the traditional heroic ending, as that would require an awakening of Virtue in himself, which he does not want to do.  So he continues to insist that true heroism is impossible, it's a suckered game.

Yet he can't do the tragic ending, as that would require catharsis, which he cannot deliver.

He could have everyone die and it all be for nought, but that neuter the series by rendering it all pointless.

⁠​⁠​⁠​⁠@maxicornejo9675 Always leave it to the internet to take a positive comment about one thing and immediately try to turn it into something negative, about a different topic. 

I didn’t say anything about Harry Potter being “on the same level” as anything else—whatever that even means. I just said that seeing how difficult it is for authors to finish long book series makes me appreciate Rowling even more for managing to finish hers on time.

Also, you can’t chalk it all up to the greater complexity of Martin’s series relative to Rowling’s, because Martin managed to finish all the earlier entries in his series in 5 years or less—with some of them taking less than 2 years. So the fact that the sixth book has taken 14+ years and still not done is clearly a sign of something else taking place.

Take your pointless negativity somewhere else.

They're nowhere near the same level - Potter is leagues ahead, by simple merit of being written by an author who could keep their creative impulses in check, pairing setting and central characters in such a way as to impose an inherent time limit on the narrative, and thus maintaining a manageable scope to the project from start to finish. By contrast, GRRM will go down in history as a failure and a cautionary whale about over-expansion - he was tipped to knock Tolkien off his throne, and instead became the ten most hated people in the fantasy genre.

His financial security could be a detriment to his motivation. I'm seeing this in pro baseball, pro football...

Guys amass fortunes -- tens of millions to hundreds of millions -- and they don't feel like doing all the year-long working out and training that is required for an American professional athlete to perform at his best.

Plus, they're saving their bodies a ton of future problems. Especially football. If you don't come out of your career brain damaged, you'll still have massive problems with your joints as you get older.

Sucks for the sport but I can't blame a 30 year-old guy worth $250 million deciding to retire.

replies.author: @spracketskooch
replies.text:
​@Nguyễn02Sakurai Now, see, that's a better criticism. I would say the moral grey in a lot of his characters, is there to demonstrate that war forces people to make awful decisions. I don't think all of his characters are morally grey either. Ned wasn't morally grey, in fact most of the Starks aren't morally grey. A lot of the conflict surrounding their family stems from their refusal to bend their morals. To me it's just a slightly more realistic depiction of war and politics in a medieval fantasy setting than other works like The Wheel of Time, or Tolkien's universe.

Tolkien for example is writing about grand cosmic forces, good vs evil per se. Martin focuses more on the human level struggle, and leaves the cosmic stuff a mystery.

Yes, that does mean that after all the pain and suffering caused by a war for power, barely anything changes. Just like what often happens in real life. Ask the population of a war torn country how they feel after the war is over, and the most common answer is "relieved that the suffering is finally over". Not victory, not glory, not depression, just relief.

I interpret that line to mean that true heroes are few and far between, and that world is full of guys who pretend to be, or are depicted to be true heros, when in reality they are did things that are truly unheroic. More of a complaint that true heroes are always shouted down and killed by the villainous scum that surround them, than no heroes exist. Even out of context that's how the line reads to me.

Think of the entire series from an anti-war frame, and it makes much more sense.

I understand your general complaint though. Many times I've complained to friends and family, can't a story just have a good ending? Does everything have to be depressing and dark all the time? I just think that Martin's writing doesn't fit that description, and the fatigue with dark, nihilistic stories comes from poor imitations of Martin's work which feature darkness for darkness' sake. There is no greater point being made. Whereas Martin uses darkness to highlight the destructive nature of war, how it tramples over the things that are actually sacred, family, community, duty, integrity, love, innocence, etc.

replies.author: @spracketskooch
replies.text:
​@severedfate8393 Well, we just disagree on this point then. I feel almost nothing for Gollum, even though it was explicitly written that he was tortured for information about the hobbits. I feel orders of magnitude more for Theon Greyjoy. Tolkien's work makes me think, and Martin's work makes me feel. 

I don't see how encouraging the reader to imagine torture is any better than describing it. Now, instead of passively reading about it, I'm actively imagining it.

You're probably right about that. Which seems backwards to me. It's like society is saying, "hey boys go out and fight in war, do horrific things, and have them done to you, but fictional accounts of these same deeds will not be tolerated". One is clearly worse as it actually happened, and it is glorified in literature and entertainment. That's just a jaded society with the wrong priorities.

I'm not familiar with Howard or Leiber, but I've read everything that Tolkien and Lovecraft have written. I still appreciate some modern, or at least more modern authors. Herbert, Martin, Robert Jordan, Brandon Sanderson, Dan Simmons. 

I suspect we're just going to have to agree to disagree and go our separate ways here.

replies.author: @a.nonimus6705
replies.text:
Tolkien was also alive during a different time. You sound like someone who is 65+ that can't understand "what's wrong with these darn youngsters these days". The world has changed a lot since Tolkien. Most importantly, the publishing industry has changed a lot. There are a lot more authors now than there were in Tolkien's days. The competition is immense. The parameters of what publishers deem "publishable" has shifted. I'm an author myself and I can guarantee you that if Tolkien submitted his manuscript today, it would be rejected. Is that a good thing? No, probably not. But it's reality. The reality that George, and myself, need to live in and adapt to. So, I don't entirely blame him. I sort of understand what he's going through, albeit I am not a multi-millionaire with Hollywood movie contracts...

EDIT: To elaborate on why Tolkien would be rejected, here's a critical component: The Fellowship of the Ring takes about 5 chapters to actually get started. Nowadays, you have 10 pages—IF you have an extraordinarily compelling hook (which, to be frank, LOTR does not have)—before the editor shreds your work. An editor today would barely make it past Tolkien's introductory descriptions before concluding that his language is too flowery, his pacing is too slow, and his focus is too disconnected

author: @philipjohn1254
text:
I called it back in 2016: The Winds of Winter was never coming. The writing was on the wall a long time ago — we just didn’t want to see it. George had already blown past multiple deadlines, even claiming in 2015 that the book was only months away. A Dance with Dragons made it obvious the story had sprawled out of control right when it needed to start converging. Then the show gave away most of his big twists, and George himself admitted he loses interest once he knows the ending.

And let’s be honest — he’s an old man who’s made millions and can enjoy life with unfettered wealth. What real chance did Winds ever have in all honesty?

replies.author: @gerudoking3180
replies.text: I'm not really sure if there's lack of morality in GoT... the whole story is a strong meditation on the partiality of our perceptions, and how our personal judgments hardly reconcile with the whole truth of a matter. If it ended with a literal "lack" of morality, that would drive the theme of the story home concerning the cyclical nature of violence/oppression on a political scale. I doubt it's "cowardliness," just laziness and lack of commitment.

replies.author: @gerudoking3180
replies.text: @Oxnafyes, of course I agree. But therein lies the initial draw to the series. The fact THAT it’s lacking the morality is the widespread appeal. Martin intentionally plays on your conscience before sweeping the leg. That makes the story the exception that proves the rule. It works because the theme is implicitly discussing the necessity for morality. Martin himself has a Catholic background, and much of the book critiques the validity to that very moral code. Since the majority of America is Catholic or has had Catholic values culturally instilled in them, the book, again, meditates on the theme of absolute truth in a world that refuses to accept it. If the story ends with a material victory, that doesn’t make the ending “hollow,” it just drives the point of the narrative home, that victory without morality IS itself meaningless. This is a message that both the top 1% grossly need (for their poor policy increases the wage gap and oppressed the basic survival needs of most of humanity) and the poor (who believe that they would act differently in a state of inherent wealth.)

He never did, the mystery and politics kept me going but eventually I stopped caring because the mystery/intrigue died in a morass of dozens of characters with too few scenes between them in too disparate locations like they were from a dozen different books that could have been good jammed together. And the politics fell apart when it became impossible to tell how who would even win a battle because nothing whatsoever was clear about the power of any army and even the occasional numbers we did get meant nothing(ex: how many men did Stanis assault King's landing with? I have no idea because it was a couple thousand from sell swords and dragon stone plus an entirely unknown amount taken from his brother's 80,000 man army).

replies.author: @akserver1
replies.text: @ComicRaptor8850 Aside from his namecalling, you have to concede that ASOIAF is nihilistic. In fact, as nihilistic as Nietzschean philosophy. Cercei's "power is power" encapsulates GRRM's worldview as well as that of the left and Communism. That doctrine, which is basically might makes right implies Social Darwinism and the Survival of the Fittest. Instead of taking offense at it, were I you, I would try to see whether nihilism is actually good and healthy for my life and make the necessary changes. Life is not a competition. We, by viewing it so make it so. Be default it isn't so. Spoiler alert! In actual fact it's not. Having said that, leading a life based on moral principles doesn't mean life is easy or that you are perfect. Au contraire! It simply means that you don't let your past mistakes define and choose your path and future for you. It simply means acceptance of reality and our shortcomings as humans. It means growth. Accepting that we are fallible does not mean identifying with the mistakes that brought as low. It implies the humility and strength to admit what is true. GRRM embodies Ego above all. All Egos are doomed to fail. GRRM didn't start with a purpose. He started with a selfish desire and pride to prove himself worthier and better than Tolkien (out of a sense of spite towards him). The grandeur of Tolkien is all due to his dedication and work. And no, nobody is engaging in worshipping Tolkien, who as a member of the human race, is by definition imperfect. People simply admire work, dedication and anything that inspires us to be better. GRRM is the quintessential toddler who sees a child happy with his toy and he bitches and moans and demand's the other's toy. GRRM believes in power. He wielded the power he accumulated to better his life materially but he ended realising that it is unfulfilling. He knows he is a shitty character and he knows that people also know that he is a shitty person who sold everybody in his pursuit of power and wealth.

replies.author: @s0uldr4ke
replies.text:
I want to make clear that I 100% agree with you in your criticism of GRRM but your example is horrible. 
1. Harry Potter is simply a much more shallow series by comparison, the meaningful characters and their interactions are heavily reduced by the setting and there is layered world building (where new powers and artifacts are introduced each book with very few repitition) instead of interactive world building where decisions influence other characters to act and think, that is a big difference. Also Harry Potter is a very one character focused series.
2. Being fast doesn't mean you are doing a good job and it says nothing about the quality if the series is finished early quite the opposite actually. To give two examples from other media I will name Hunter x Hunter as well as Berserk. Two series that have been going for over 20 years (one sadly canonically ended prematurely by the death of it's author even though the team does still write chapters based on his notes to finish) and constantly created chapters even though due to helath related issues both series went on year long hiatuses and had to reduce the published chapters each year... by a lot.

The issue aren't hiatuses but bad planning as well as the open lying GRRM does.
From a writing pov it is easy and I mean really easy to create a world with many characters and constantly expand on it... the true challenge is to tie it back together in a way that reading certain characters does not feel like a waste of time. It is a challenge only a few authors are able to accomplish (two of which I have used as an example for that reason) and GRRM isn't an author of this magniude and because he also understands the implications of not being able to tie the story back together in a meaningful way he prefers to keep the story itself unfinished avoiding the admission...that is the true crime. If it was just people having to wait every 5-8 years for a book to come out you better believe fans won't take issue with that.

author: @dcarr505
text: He has some nerve being upset with fans making snarky comments about the long wait for his books…… HE was the one who originally announced he was doing a seven book series and by doing so, HE set the expectations……NOT the fans. The fans of his work were game enough to sign on and support his grand vision, to continue to throw money at him to keep it coming and to grant him the lavish lifestyle and fame that he enjoys so much today. The loyal FANS were the ones who made him the success he is today…..and he thinks they don’t deserve his respect or his dedication to the book series goals HE set for HIMSELF from the beginning? Talk about ingratitude! He could have easily just said, “Hey, I’m starting a new fantasy book project….and if it is well received, I may do some more sequels to it….” and not made mention of any set number of books he had in mind……that would have saved him and the fans a lot of aggravation….there would not have been the goal of seven books that had to be met. HE is the only one he has to blame for the situation he finds himself in.

replies.text:
I think fans would be willing to accept the Bran ending.

It needed to be set up a LOT more, which the show didn’t do.  Bran did nothing to earn the throne in the show.  The whole “being able to tell the world’s stories” wasn’t fleshed out AT ALL.  “Let’s make Bran king” came entirely out of left field, which made it impossible to swallow, when Jon Snow was clearly being set up by the show to take the throne.

Presumably, had GRRM actually written the damn books, there would have been a lot more about Bran, and we would have learned more of the extents of his abilities, and why he should be the new king.  Give us that set up, and we’d accept it, maybe even like it!

HBO, Weiss & Benioff fucked up the rest of the series for the entire world by disappointing the fans so hard that the AUTHOR even gave up on the story.

replies.text: What exactly is postmodernist about his writing? There are heroes and villains, good and evil. It's just that the heroes aren't perfect, and the good and evil resides in the hearts of men rather than being represented by an external force. Truth exists and is objective. One example being that a major theme of his writing is war is a pointless hell. Meaning exists and is there to be discovered rather than created. Family, friendship, loyalty, and integrity are often highlighted as being the truly important things. History and culture carry meaning. I'm not convinced that you've actually read any of his writing. If you have, then it wasn't even at a surface level.

replies.author: @crusaderpenguin5326
replies.text: This isn't remotely accurate and is a butchering of the books to plug your "postmodernism bad" stance. Martin set out for a realistic (in the realm of fantasy, anyway), gritty tale where heroes and villains are IMPERFECT, not that they don't exist. There are still heroes, there are still villains. There are still payoffs for these characters (i.e. why was everyone mad that the show didn't have Jon Snow kill the night king? Clearly there was a heroic arc there). It's just more grey and nuanced than perfect good and abhorrent evil and there's no clear cop-out religion to make things obvious. Sometimes you'll root for a villain because they're against a villain you detest more. Sometimes heroes will come into conflict with each other. I hate to say it, but life IS morally gray like that, it's realistic. That doesn't mean Tolkien's works are bad, hell they're some of my favorite books ever. But it's absurd to say that a morally gray story where people can die indiscriminately is the cause of Martin not being able to finish the books. There have been dozens of gritty, morally gray series that have been finished satisfactorily. What a pathetic way to shoehorn in a political point. I dislike postmodernism as well, but you're not doing your message any favors with this blatant misrepresentation.

replies.author: @crusaderpenguin5326
replies.text: @mladabitanga2677 Taking things in a more realistic direction does not equal a "leftist mockery". Are you serious right now? George himself loves Tolkien's works, he just wanted to tell a dark fantasy tale. Like Berserk, or the Black Company, or Malazan, etc. You know that dark fantasy exists right? You know that there can be stories where characters are split into more than "good" and "evil", right? Fuck man, if this is how you view dark fantasy I truly pity you. Tolkien is amazing, but people have different stories they want to tell. Some of those stories are more gritty and realistic in nature.

replies.text:
​​@AnonymousNoNaymeWhat additional proof would you like? More examples? How about Robb: it's not his heroic goal that screws him over, it's him throwing away an alliance for feelings. There's no cynical postmodernism there, Robb's noble goal of avenging his father was likely to succeed if he didn't screw up his alliance. His heroism doesn't cost him anything. His stupidity does. There's nothing postmodern about stupidity screwing you over. That's just realism.

If anything, one might say Robb's downfall was because of an UNHEROIC action. Robb chose feelings over duty to his father and duty to his men. Tolkien valued duty quite heavily. If Tolkien had written the exact same tale you would praise it as a warning for why one should not abandon their duties. That enough for you?

author: @doc_adams8506
text: I posted my doubts before the pandemic. All you have to do is examine the facts: age, length between published works, self-confessed ADD, sudden extreme wealth. Those factors do nothing for motivate someone wanting to finish a book.

replies.author: @racheljones2944
replies.text:
And let’s throw in: an excessively and increasingly complicated plot- from an author who likens himself to a “gardener” rather than an “architect”, meaning that he prefers to do minimal planning, and instead “find out as it grows”.  Is that a bad way to write?  Not inherently.  But in this case I think the story got hopelessly out of control a long time ago, and GRRM simply has no idea how to fix it (and has lost interest in doing so anyway).

Now, the most popular explanation I see these days is that the reception of the HBO series’ ending (which IIRC he did supply) scared him off finishing the books- but that doesn’t track with all the years of no-shows and obvious stalling *before* that.  I believe GRRM gave up completely at some point in the 2010s, but can’t admit it for both face-saving and legal reasons.  He’s probably decided his best option is to just keep stringing his fans and publishers along for the remainder of his mortal existence.  I can’t even say he’s wrong about that.
